text: It was incredible to work with Irfan sir.
Yeah.
And I felt like I met some Sufi soul.
He was just beautiful inside out.
Thank you so much for coming to our studio.
They'll get very excited.
Thank you.
No, it's my pleasure.
I've been an ardent reader for a very long time.
Yeah.
And I love all the stories.
I think it really connects you to people.
And we have millions of stories in India like that.
You guys are doing a great job.
Thank you so much.
And people in office are very excited.
As you can see, it's like a live set up.
I love the office.
Nobody has any real work here.
So you have a great fan following
and we're excited to hear your story.
No, thank you.
Thank you.
So we were just wondering, we were mapping your journey.
We were doing research.
And you've had such a vast amount of experience
in terms of not just a film industry,
but you started off by studying business management.
Of course, your childhood days, you liked filmmaking
and all of those things.
But you studied business management.
And then you even got a job as a business manager in Dubai.
Right.
And then you ended up in films.
Then from the South to Bollywood.
What do you think is that one thing that
enabled you to find your calling or your purpose?
I think it's the constant searching.
So I grew up around all these kids from business families.
And I was always a bit of an outsider
because we moved to Chennai.
I wasn't born there.
So to kind of fit in with the kids I went to school with,
I had to be a part of their conversations.
And so I started reading Business Line and Financial Times
because invariably there would be some discussion.
And I think just I went to school
with some really great kids.
And so I was always trying to be a part
of different conversations.
So like educating myself.
And I never thought I had a chance in movies
because traditionally in my realm
there was no sort of concept of second generation actors
having made it.
So I was like, that is not an option.
So the next thing that I was exposed to
was this business all over.
And so then I decided everybody starting
management, so I'm going to do the same thing.
But even there I realized that it's marketing
and the creative side that was exciting for me.
Be on a point I thought numbers seem very black and white.
And I think movies are so much color.
So subconsciously somewhere I was seeking it.
And I kept trying to stay as far away as possible
because I had it fixed in my head
that anything I do in movies is going to be
such a great comparison with my dad.
And I would definitely ruin his name or something
because he's had such a stellar reputation
as not just an actor, just how he conducts himself.
And I was like, I don't want to be that kid
who messes it up.
But I think working nine to five,
it was sort of mundane.
I wasn't really motivated to go to work.
But when I would do these short films
with some close friends, it never felt like work.
I didn't care if I slept, I didn't care if I ate.
So I think that's when I realized my calling
has to be in cinema, irrespective of what I do.
I want to be around a film set.
I found it really dynamic.
There's so many people that participate
in creating what we see.
So yeah, eventually I think my biggest fear was that,
I would turn 40 and regret not having tried.
And I just turned 40, so I'm glad I tried.
And how did the, at what age did the shift happen?
Like saying, okay, let me give this a shot.
I was like 26, 27.
I was in Dubai, I was working there.
I was doing multiple things.
I had a company in construction,
I was doing some consulting, I had an ad agency.
All of this I was doing and I was like,
I'm still not happy.
And I think in your 20s, I read this quote somewhere,
your 20s is the only time that you have the luxury
of not knowing what you're gonna do in life,
where you're gonna end up.
And I think somewhere along the way,
this facing your fears thing really hit me hard.
I was like, if I face it, it gives me so much more
confidence and it's not as scary as I think it is.
So I was like, so earlier I would direct to not act,
because to avoid comparison.
And I was like, what is the most scariest thing
for me to attempt in movies and it would be
to face the camera, to put myself out there.
And I was like, I wanna do it.
Wow.
And when you did do it, how did that feel?
It was scary.
It was a big like seminal moment in my life
and even for my family, I think.
So it's such an interesting dichotomy,
because on one end you have a father
who's obviously created this name,
like you were mentioning.
The second side, you finally realize that actually
this is your calling and you have to kind of
get into the pit field and deal with the comparison, right?
That would inevitably happen.
How do you overcome the fear of that comparison?
Because it's such a, it's a weight to carry.
It is.
I think the one thing for, I think my parents,
especially my father, he's like,
I can protect you from anything else,
but anything that you, I can't come and act for you.
So whatever you do on screen, you're gonna get,
you know, criticized, there'll be brick bats.
They will try to bring you down especially,
even more so because you're my son or whatever,
and I don't know how to protect you.
And so I think that was the biggest thing for,
and now I understand as a parent for him,
but I was like, you know,
I also should have my own journey.
I should also, and I need to like face it,
see where it takes me.
And it's not like it still doesn't bother me.
Like there are, there is always a fraction of people,
no matter what I do, they will still say,
oh, it's only because of your dad,
or like, you know, because of your lineage,
or else you would never be here or things like that.
And I still read them, it still irks me.
But what I've realized is I love what I do,
you know, and no one can take that away from me.
So you can say all that you want,
I'm gonna go in every day, give it my 100%
and really enjoy what I do.
So this 27, 28 year old boy slash man,
who's, you know, taking this massive career decision
of moving careers and moving into films, what then?
What was the first thing that you did?
Do you recall?
So I had that big like sort of breakdown moment
where when I told my folks, this is what I wanna do,
and I was like, I'm not happy doing what I'm doing
and I wanna give this a shot.
I know it's like life changing,
but this is what I wanna do.
But then my dad was like, I'm not gonna do anything for you.
I'm not gonna call in favors,
I'm not gonna get you opportunities.
If you wanna do this, you know, nobody helped me,
so you're gonna have to figure it out yourself.
So I moved back from Dubai to India,
and so the best thing about having siblings,
my older sister was really fundamental.
She's like, okay, you wanna do this?
You better get on it.
I'm like, but what do I do?
How do I go about it?
She's like, you've not done any kind of acting,
so how about going to acting school?
I'm like, okay.
Then she went online and then she found out courses
at Bombay and she's like, there's one starting on Monday,
and I think this is like a Wednesday or something.
She's like, I was like, what, up and go?
Like this weekend?
She's like, yeah, let's go.
You have some friends, I go stay with them, figure it out.
And I did that.
I showed up on Friday with like a demand draft
to apply for acting school.
Stayed with a friend for a month
till I found an apartment.
But all of that, again, as experiences,
I think it's something I would never trade.
I've made some great friendships from then,
which have later on become, you know,
so many things in my life,
like a friend of mine who was there in that course
now manages my endorsements.
It's weird how things span out.
And I don't think if she didn't want that day
to say, do this, I wouldn't have done it.
And then I got to meet a lot of people in Bombay.
There was some inquiries where, you know,
maybe you should try auditioning.
And then I had like a eye infection and I had to go back.
Or my career might have been a little different.
I might have probably even started out here,
even if it's doing bit roles or whatever I would have got.
But I guess there's always fate and destiny at play.
My eye infection was so serious
that I had to fly back immediately
and go to doctors in Chennai and Kochi until it was sorted.
But then I think that's how I found my first film.
I met a bunch of newcomers
who were just trying to get a film made
and they couldn't get the dates of any famous actor.
So they thought, what is the next best thing?
Maybe we can go to some actors, kids,
and maybe one of them will listen to us.
And I guess because I had no other baggage of scripts,
it was the first thing I read.
And I liked it, I liked what they were trying to make.
I loved that everybody was new.
I was like, I want to be the only new person.
So we'll all learn together, we'll all fumble,
stumble together, pick each other up.
And yeah, that's how my first film came about.
The reason I asked this question is because
so many people in their life go through this
this question in their head, right?
Like, which way do I go?
Like, my inner calling is something,
but the stability of a job, there's so much at play.
So what advice would you give people
who are in a similar phase in life, right?
That you found yourself.
Anyone having these doubts, you live only once.
Just take that leap.
I guarantee you it will change your life.
Yeah. Yeah.
Because you won't know, I mean, you can still,
I mean, you try, you fail,
and you can still go back and find that job.
Because it's not like you're no less qualified.
And you'll always have the experience to boot.
But don't not give it that shot.
Because time goes like that, you know,
it's just, if you don't give it that shot,
you can get to a certain age
and then wish you'd done it differently.
And you took the leap.
You had your first film.
What was the process or the journey after?
So then it just kind of like snowballed.
As I was working in that first film,
I was also, I think, at the right place at the right time.
There was also this gap for maybe actors
of my sort of age group in Mulyalu.
Because we had these two big superstars.
And, you know, they were also at a stage
where now they can't play kids that are out of college.
It was also that phase in cinema
where people were doing coming-of-age movies.
So I ended up becoming the poster boy for that.
And so this first four or five films that I got,
you know, I genuinely believe it was just dumb luck.
I was just seeking experiences.
I found my kind of cinema.
Your second film went on to win a National Film Award.
In a lot of people's books, that's like ticking a, you know,
like there's a checklist, there's a box that I've ticked.
But what did success mean to you?
Like what were you chasing at that point?
Once you've entered, you found your purpose
and you're doing what you love.
But what was success?
No, I think the film for me was,
it just became this amazing timeless sort of landmark film.
And I just felt really fortunate.
So even when it won that award,
I didn't think I had anything to do with it.
And I think that was the best way to look at it
because suddenly I thought, oh my God, I've arrived.
And, you know, my film won this award
and it's nationally recognized.
It messes with your judgment.
Like you can't, and then where do you go from there?
You know, and you can't find those films
every day or every year.
So which is why, like, I keep saying, I'm like, you know,
I just want to be a part of the best films of the year.
I don't care what my role is.
I always hear of the film.
So I didn't think too much about it.
I just went about, you know, working on my next film.
And to date, I know, even if I do a bad movie
or if I get a bad review, that film,
Ustad Hotel can play on TV in Kerala
and it will still endear me to the audience.
They still see me as a member of their family.
Like I'm one of the kids in the house.
Yeah.
You've also mentioned that you're very greedy as an actor.
You want your biggest fears that you'll miss out on good roles.
So how is it that you kind of operate,
given that it's not always that that can happen, right?
Correct, correct.
So is that a metric of success for you as well?
It's not about success.
I mean, I think my greed, it's not even about the roles.
You know, I've never tried to do a film
because the role really challenged me.
Obviously, there have been those kind of films,
but like I said, I keep heroing the film.
I'm like, is this something great that we're attempting?
When something stems from authentic,
sort of inspired writing and, you know,
and they don't know, you know, they're shooting in the dark,
but they're like, you know, we're going to give it everything
to try and make something special.
I am drawn to those kind of people.
I do put out those energies.
They do come to me.
And I'm not greedy about my role or my screen time.
So like, but to be,
if I think there's a special film being made,
I'm very happy with reaching out.
I'm like, hey, you know, think of me for anything.
You want me to go cameo?
Can I be a part of it in some way?
I'm happy to do it.
But like year end, you know, the best films list comes out.
If mine's not on it, I'm just like,
what am I doing with my life?
And was Bollywood something that you'd earmarked as,
okay, this is a next step in my career?
No, I don't think, I was not even thinking that far.
I was thinking very much on what am I doing next?
And what's my next film?
But I've always been kind of going with what comes my way.
I think when, when karma came my way,
I just loved everything about it.
I felt like it had soul.
I loved the people that were making it.
Again, I found some great friendships
during the course of that film.
It was incredible to work with Ilfan Sir.
And I felt like I met some Sufi soul.
He was just beautiful inside out.
Yeah.
What are some of your happiest memories
on that film set with him?
I think that he was,
I felt like he was really living every moment of his life.
He was really alive.
And he was not thinking about how this comes across,
or how he's performing.
There's no sort of fear of judgment.
He felt really free.
So even as a performer,
I know he was offered the protagonist role,
and he said, no, I want to play Shaukat.
Cause he's like, he identifies with that role.
And he can do anything at that point, right?
The way he was in his career.
And he chose to do that role.
And when I came on board,
he really stood by the fact that I'm the protagonist.
But on shoot days, you don't see him prep.
He's always there.
And he's not greedy,
or he doesn't try to overpower the other actors.
He's happy doing his thing.
He gives you a lot of space.
He gives you a lot of confidence.
And also for him, I think shooting in that whole belt
of India where we drove from Ooty to Kerala,
it was, I don't know, maybe,
it's not like shooting up north for him.
So he had a little more freedom.
So he was experiencing everything.
So I remember one day, he finished shoot a little early.
And so there's, what we call,
a Valla Kaaran,
a lone fisherman on a canoe.
And in the backwaters of the,
and we're shooting.
And then next thing I look, and he's gone off with him.
And they've gone, and they've gone fishing,
whatever, and he's come back.
So I loved all of that.
And he would ask me, he's like,
who are some of the filmmakers in Malayalam?
Do you know Mr. Srinivasan?
I've read about him when I was in film school.
I'd love to call and talk to him.
If there's some filmmakers that you know,
I'd connect with them,
and maybe I can come up with a film with them.
And I'm like, dude, you're at Valla Kaaran.
You don't have to go to anyone.
They'll come to you.
Exactly.
I don't think our filmmakers in Malayalam
were thinking that they could approach him.
So I tried my best to connect him to as many people.
And just that love for cinema,
and again, that greed to do good work,
work with good filmmakers,
irrespective of where they're from.
I was literally like a spectator on that set.
I was just watching him.
I was like, front row audience,
I have the best seats in the house.
I'm just gonna watch this star perform.
What's the biggest thing you learned from him?
I think that where he was living every moment
to the fullest,
and seeking experiences,
and seeking,
and not wanting any validation of any sort,
and just being that free.
Yeah.
And is that something that you've managed?
I still, I think I still have a while,
not a while to go, it's more like,
I should be less harsh on myself.
I think I'm overly critical.
I think I'm very bogged down by, you know,
criticism and what people say.
It does drive me.
I think I'm always out to prove the tractor's wrong.
But to be that free
would be something that I would love to get to.
And are you a spiritual person?
Do you engage in a reflection,
or is that something that you don't?
I think as you get older, it finds you.
Okay.
You know, because when you're younger,
you want stability in life.
You want to find your calling in life.
You want to find your career.
You want to find a life partner.
You want, you know, peace at home.
You want to help your family and stuff.
But then as you get older that changes, right?
Like, you know?
And I try to navigate through life.
I do feel like I,
I think this is the stuff I read maybe.
Or, like, the knowledge I seek out is,
is, how do I do this as healthy as possible?
How do I
maintain my mental health?
My peace.
I asked you this question because
we came across something you said about your wife.
where you said there was almost like a celestial sign.
Yeah.
That you should marry her.
I guess I do. I mean, I've not maybe
broken it down.
I have my faith.
I believe in God.
I'm moderately religious.
But I do look for signs.
Weirdly, like
the number three for some reason, if
not in the sense of numerology, but if something comes to me
in three years.
Three times they asked me, I'm like, maybe there's something there.
Maybe I'm supposed to do this film.
We went to the same school, but never
as she was way my junior.
When I was in the twelfth, she was like
in the seventh or something, little kid,
so it would be really wrong to look at her like that.
But years later, obviously, like I was
so all of this happened in the same year, this finding my first film
and meeting her. So it's like in one year, my life just flipped.
Everything aligned.
Everything aligned, I guess. Yeah.
So then I saw her out three times randomly.
And I'm like, why does she keep popping up?
We're both from Chennai and I never see this girl out.
And suddenly in the span of that one week,
I saw her out thrice. I'm like...
Intervention. Intervention.
So I was like, again, like, you know, taking a leap of faith.
What's the worst that can happen?
She might not be interested.
So then how did you...
Oh, I did it old school. I sent her a message on Facebook.
Okay. I was like, hey.
So I literally worded it like that. I was like,
hey, I know, like, you know, they're looking to get you married.
They're looking to get me married.
Do you want to meet for a cup of coffee and see if there's something there?
How sweet. How refreshing.
Yeah. And I was like, if there isn't,
we'll just be two schoolmates who met randomly and that's fine.
And she was like, okay, sure. Let's grab a cup of coffee.
And yeah.
And I almost, I think, knew from the time I started seeing her out.
So I think I went into that coffee being like
pitching this whole idea to her.
So I think she was quite amused by it.
So the idea was that I've seen you three times.
I didn't even know that. I was like, hey, you know,
we're from Chennai, we're from the same school,
we have so many things in common, we have so many friends.
You know, I think this could go somewhere.
And yeah. And I guess she was really young.
I don't think she knew what she was getting into.
I wasn't an actor then.
And so I think, like my mum
always says she married a lawyer.
And that if my dad is an actor, she wouldn't have married him.
And I don't think my wife knew what it means to be married to an actor.
They have no connection to the film industry whatsoever.
And it's been difficult in the sense that
the amount of time I'm away from home and how much she holds fort.
It's familiar to me because I've seen my mother do it.
But I know it was new for her and she was really young when we got married.
But she's done a stellar job.
She's really held it together. She's always been supportive.
But it is tough to be married to actors.
We're like gypsies.
But how do you manage that balancing act, right?
Especially since you've become a father.
And then that role obviously becomes more important.
Because it's your need, right?
It's your need more than your child, I think.
Because kids adapt.
But it's like I have so much form of everything
that happens with her.
Every...
Taking her first step or speaking her first word.
And I want to be there for everything.
I want to be that regular dad that goes and picks her up from school.
Or is there for like parent-teacher meetings.
Because my dad could never make it for all that.
And so I personally...
I put in the effort.
And I can literally see the difference in my child every time I leave.
And I come back.
I can see her cheeks are going down.
Or like her legs are looking longer.
So that's tough.
That is tough.
And it's such a parallel universe, right?
Like when you're at home with this family man.
And then you're on set.
You were quoted as the most desirable man.
Which I've never understood.
But it was there.
You must be having crazy fan encounters.
Any that come to your mind?
It's very sweet actually.
So it's never been like crazy, crazy per se.
But I do receive a lot of love.
And I'm very grateful for it.
And how has it been for your wife?
Because she married somebody who had not even done...
I mean was about to do his first film.
I don't think she ever fully accepted that I'm all of these things.
Which is great because...
How does she react to most desirable matches?
She doesn't give it much importance.
She's like, I know what you're like at home.
Like you're groomed.
You have a team and they do your hair and all this stuff.
At home I'm just beached on a couch.
You know, I'm scruffy.
So she's like, this is the most desirable man right here.
Take a photo.
But it's good. I mean I think she's always there to keep me grounded.
So what are you like with friends?
What's a typical day with friends?
I love to converse.
Like I love to just have conversations.
It can be the same stuff over and over again.
It can be the same jokes we cracked in school.
And I never get tired of it.
If we're in Kochi with my parents.
I think my folks are also a lot more...
My dad likes young people.
More than people his age.
He's like...
He's always engaged with better, younger people.
I think that's why he stays so young.
So it's always a full house.
There's always people there.
We're all just drinking coffee.
Eating a good meal and just talking about everything.
And the same thing.
I've moved to Chennai now for my daughter's school.
And I like a full house.
And my daughter's always like, is nobody coming today?
That's true.
So yeah, it's easy. I like it like that.
I do love to travel with friends.
I have some set groups that I travel with.
Because we're good travelers.
You don't get on.
No, when you travel you realize, oh my God, my friend's a jackass.
He's very difficult to travel with.
You know, there are people who can't agree on...
what to eat, where to eat.
They just say no to everything.
You waste so much time doing things like that.
And how's it like with your father now?
That now it's actor to actor.
When you're in maybe perhaps discussion of your role.
I think when I was younger, I think he had the same fear as me
that I might not amount to anything.
So I think now...
I think he's quietly proud.
He doesn't really say things to my face.
But it's there.
I can tell him certain things that he says
or conversations he has with his close groups.
I can tell that he's very proud.
And now we speak very...
as friends and as equals.
Which is the most delightful thing.
So even now, if I go back home to Kochi...
And I sometimes go for work.
I sometimes go for brand shoots or if I'm shooting a film.
And my dad's always calling.
And then he suddenly switches to dad mode.
He's no longer actor.
He's like, when are you coming home?
I'm like, they said 8pm.
At 8pm, he'll be like, it's 8 o'clock.
It might be another hour or two.
Like 10 is like, where are you?
Because the only thing he wants to do is for us to come back
and just sit on the dining table and just...
We finish dinner and just talk.
And he just loves to catch up with me
till we literally are all falling asleep.
That's so sweet.
And that's I think his big joy now.
Just sitting and catching up with me.
So I do try to find that time.
But I think for us boys to get to that stage with our fathers,
especially in India, it's a big...
I feel like it's kind of an accomplishment.
Yeah, it's a moment.
It's a moment, yeah.
So I'm genuinely enjoying that and very...
Yeah, it's just...
I can't ask for more.
And we were discussing offline.
I just saw the trailer to Guns and Gulabs.
And it looks amazing.
It looks so wild and the one-liners are very catchy.
How was that experience working with Raj and DK, Raj Kumar?
So many talented.
It was amazing.
I mean, I think everybody who's seen the trailer is like,
it is such a fun ride.
I think shooting it was like that.
I think Raj and DK are great guys.
They're so fun to work with.
But also, they're so good at what they do.
You know, so it's very collaborative.
You're very at ease.
You know, they're very open to improvisation.
Like, you can have suggestions, you can try things
and they will listen to everything.
But, you know, as directors, they know where to take it,
when to take good ideas.
And just really sharp, really witty,
I think a lot of the humor is them.
And that whole world, I think when I heard the idea,
I kind of fell in love with this world they've created,
this whole fictional town called Gulabganj.
But what I wasn't prepared for when I got to set
was all of the amazing actors.
You know, of course there's Raj Kumar, there's Gulshan,
and Adarsh, and all of them, and Satish Koshik,
but every supporting character,
every... when you walk into a frame,
everyone is so convincing, so believable.
I didn't feel like they were in costume or hair and make, nothing.
I was like, it's like time travel, man.
I've been transported to...
Like a match tree there.
Yeah, to like 89, 90, and I'm there.
I could fully believe those scenes.
I just had to react.
And, you know, like if I prep my lines enough,
not think of my lines, and I fully was alive in that show
because of just the world creation. It was amazing.
And are you attached to the character?
I love that role.
I mean, I know it's an ensemble, and, you know,
sometimes people are like, you know,
why aren't you just playing the protagonist?
I'm like, I play the protagonist and everything.
I loved this character, Adrind's role.
I think when you watch the show,
you don't know where he's going.
It's great.
It's like erratic.
Yeah, and it's got a whole family angle to it,
which I really enjoyed.
I have a 16-year-old daughter on the show.
Oh, wow.
And so I was very curious about,
oh, when Mariam turns 16, like what do I speak to her about?
And, you know, how does showing my affection to my six-year-old
and when she's 16 change as a father?
